content.text: Next.js is a full-stack React framework maintained by Vercel. Where Vite and CRA produce single-page applications that run entirely in the browser, Next.js adds a server layer that can render pages on the server, fetch data before a page is sent to the client, and expose API endpoints — all in one project. It is the recommended way to build production React applications when you need SSR, file-based routing, or backend logic alongside your UI.

attrs.alt: Next.js App Router request lifecycle: Server Components fetch data and emit zero JS; Client Components are hydrated; file-system routing maps app/ folders to URLs
attrs.title: Next.js App Router architecture

content.text: SSR/SSG produces HTML bots can read
Blog / marketing site with static content
SSG: pages built once, served from CDN
Route Handlers or Server Actions replace a separate Express backend
Next.js targets web browsers, use Expo
. Run it with npx so you always get the latest version:

npx create-next-app@latest my-app

# Prompts:
# ✔ Would you like to use TypeScript? … Yes
# ✔ Would you like to use ESLint? … Yes
# ✔ Would you like to use Tailwind CSS? … Yes
# ✔ Would you like your code inside a `src/` directory? … Yes
# ✔ Would you like to use App Router? (recommended) … Yes
# ✔ Would you like to use Turbopack for next dev? … Yes
# ✔ Would you like to customize the import alias (@/*)? … No

cd my-app
npm run dev   # starts at http://localhost:3000
Skip the interactive prompts by passing flags directly:

my-app/
├── src/
│   └── app/
│       ├── layout.tsx       ← root layout (html + body, shared across all pages)
│       ├── page.tsx         ← /  (the home page)
│       ├── globals.css
│       ├── about/
│       │   └── page.tsx     ← /about
│       └── api/
│           └── hello/
│               └── route.ts ← GET /api/hello
├── public/                  ← static files served at /
├── next.config.ts
├── tsconfig.json
├── package.json
└── .env.local

are invisible to the router and can hold shared code, components, or utilities.
Next.js has two routing systems. New projects should use the App Router.
In the App Router every component is a Server Component by default. Server Components run only on the server: they can be
, call databases directly, and never send their implementation code to the browser. Add
at the top of a file to opt that subtree into client rendering — necessary whenever you use

A Server Component can import and render a Client Component. The reverse is also possible (passing Server Components as children props through a Client Component), but a Server Component cannot be imported inside a Client Component file directly.
The URL structure is derived entirely from the
folder tree. Special filenames control layout, loading, and error handling at each route segment:

Shown instantly while page.tsx is loading (automatic Suspense boundary)

Layout components are Server Components by default. Adding
to a layout converts it and all its children to client rendering — put state in a separate wrapper component instead.

Next.js maintains several cache layers. Understanding them prevents stale data bugs:
Deduplicates identical fetch() calls within one render tree
Automatic per request; cannot be disabled
fetch() responses on the server, persisted across requests
Static HTML + RSC payload rendered at build time
ISR revalidate, on-demand revalidation, or redeploy
RSC payloads in the browser for visited/prefetched routes
file at the project root to run code before a request reaches any route. Common uses: auth guards, geolocation redirects, A/B testing header injection.

Next.js compiles TypeScript with SWC (not tsc), so the type-check step does not block the dev server. Run
in CI to catch type errors. Key tsconfig options that create-next-app sets:
Correct resolution for Next.js / webpack imports
SWC handles JSX; TSC leaves it untouched
